Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent routine, honestly. Being 26 and having been through some schooling before, I know myself well enough to block out about three hours a day for studying. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often set up my materials at the kitchen table where I could spread everything out and physically work through problems. The challenge was that without internet at home, I had to plan my online work around when I could get to the library or a coffee shop with wifi.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, usually mornings before things got hectic, but reality didn't always cooperate. When deadlines piled up, my whole schedule would shift and I'd find myself cramming in study time wherever I could. The stress of juggling everything meant some weeks were way more intense than others, even though I tried to stay steady.
Q3: Usually it was assignment deadlines that pushed me to log in. Since I don't have internet at home, every login was pretty intentional—I'd make a list of what I needed to do and then head somewhere with wifi. Sometimes the gamified tasks would motivate me too because they felt less overwhelming than the regular coursework.
Q4: Not really a daily routine, but I developed a rhythm of going to the library on certain days specifically for LMS work. It became my 'online day' where I'd batch all my digital tasks together. That pattern emerged out of necessity more than anything, but it helped me feel somewhat organized despite the access limitations.
Q5: The video content was actually really valuable for me—I watched a lot of it, like hours worth, because I could replay demonstrations and really see how things worked. As a kinesthetic learner, seeing processes in action helped me understand better than just reading. The gamified tasks also felt useful because they broke things into smaller, more manageable pieces that didn't feel as overwhelming.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums, to be honest. With only six posts the whole course, I just couldn't keep up with ongoing conversations when I only had sporadic internet access. By the time I'd log in, discussions had moved on and it felt pointless to contribute. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because I was already struggling to complete the core assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to what I'd actually need to know for exams or real-world application. The gamified elements helped because earning points or badges gave me immediate feedback that I was making progress. If something felt abstract or disconnected, my motivation—which is already pretty low—just couldn't sustain the effort.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. With my assignment completion being pretty low and stress being high, I had to prioritize what was required just to pass. Required activities came with consequences, so they got my attention even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff, even if interesting, fell off my radar completely.
Q9: Absolutely, this was actually most of my studying. I'd download everything I could during my library sessions—PDFs, video files when possible, lecture notes—and then study them at home without internet. I'd print things out and write all over them, make flashcards, work through problems by hand. That tactile, physical engagement is how I actually learn best.
Q10: Most of my real learning happened offline at my kitchen table with printed materials and handwritten notes spread everywhere. I'd spend hours working through concepts physically, but the LMS would just show that one download session. My 21 hours of weekly studying barely registered online because I was doing the actual work disconnected from the platform.
Q11: All the time—that was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything available for the next week or two, then work through it all offline. The videos I watched were often downloaded versions I'd replay multiple times at home. The LMS probably showed me accessing a video once, but I might have watched it three or four times offline.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when multiple courses had overlapping deadlines. My stress levels were really high and my resilience isn't great, so I'd hit these walls where everything felt impossible. Without peer interactions to lean on and with my anxiety creeping up, some weeks I just went through the motions without really absorbing anything.
Q13: Hugely. When I was overwhelmed or exhausted, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities—work, life stuff—meant I sometimes had to choose between completing assignments and actually understanding the material. The fatigue was real, and it showed in my assignment completion rate being so low even though I was putting in study hours.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos or readings and technically be 'engaging' with them, but my mind would be elsewhere worrying about deadlines or just too tired to focus. The watch time might look impressive, but some of those minutes were me rewinding because I'd zoned out completely. Being present physically doesn't mean being present mentally.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was the internet access issue—I'd plan to do something but then not make it to the library. Part of it was anxiety making tasks feel bigger than they were, so I'd avoid them until the deadline forced my hand. My assignment completion rate reflects this pattern pretty clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation since my internal motivation is pretty low. I'd plan my library trips around due dates, sometimes cutting it really close. The pressure of deadlines got me to actually submit things, but it also meant I wasn't always doing my best work—just whatever I could manage in time.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but my response time to engage with it was slow—like almost thirteen hours on average. Sometimes feedback came when I didn't have internet access, so I couldn't act on it immediately. Positive feedback from the gamified tasks gave me little boosts, but critical feedback on assignments sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid looking at it.
Q18: The course structure with 17 online components was honestly overwhelming for someone without reliable internet. I appreciated that there were gamified elements that broke things down, but the sheer volume of online courses made it hard to keep up. The high attendance requirement was manageable, but balancing that with all the online work was a constant struggle.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they felt achievable and gave me quick wins when everything else felt like drowning. But the heavy reliance on online discussion and peer interaction was harder for me given my access limitations and the fact that I had zero peer interactions recorded. The course assumed consistent internet access that I just didn't have.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in, watching videos, exploring resources, but then life gets in the way of actually completing and submitting work. High activity might mean they're trying, they're present, they're engaging with materials—but converting that into finished assignments is a different challenge entirely. Stress, confusion, or competing priorities can create that gap.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score and final grade show I passed despite relatively low LMS engagement levels. A lot of my actual learning happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten practice. Someone could know the material well from independent study but not have the digital footprint to prove it.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long files are open. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm actually understanding the video I'm watching or if I've downloaded something and studied it extensively offline. They capture presence but not comprehension or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and apply what I'm learning. It's not just going through motions or logging hours—it's when I'm genuinely curious, working through problems, making connections. For me as a kinesthetic learner, engagement often looks like physically doing something with the material, which doesn't always happen on a screen.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with access issues and how creative I had to be to work around them. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent at my kitchen table with printed materials, genuinely learning. It also wouldn't show my emotional state—the stress, the anxiety, the moments of feeling happy when something finally clicked. My engagement was real, just largely invisible to the system.
